title: Accessibility in HTML
summary: Accessibility means making pages usable by everyone, including people with disabilities.
Good HTML is accessible by default when you use it correctly.
Alt text, labels, and semantic elements all help.
It also helps everyone, not only people with disabilities.

sections.text: Not everyone uses a website the same way. Some people cannot see the screen, some cannot use a mouse. Accessibility is about building pages that work for all of them, and good HTML does most of the work for you.

sections.title: What Accessibility Means
sections.text: Accessibility means everyone can use your page, including people who are blind, have low vision, or cannot use a mouse. Many use a screen reader, a tool that reads the page aloud. Your HTML decides how well that tool can do its job.

sections.title: How HTML Helps
sections.text: You have already met the tools. Alt text describes images. Labels name form fields. Semantic elements like nav and main mark the parts of the page. Used correctly, these make a page accessible without any extra effort.

media.alt: A screen reader reading a well structured page aloud

sections.title: A Building with Ramps
sections.text: An accessible building has ramps, wide doors, and clear signs. They help people in wheelchairs, but they also help someone pushing a stroller or carrying boxes. Accessible HTML is the same. Built for some, better for all.

media.alt: A ramp at a building entrance used by many different people

sections.title: Simple Accessibility Wins
items: Give every image a clear alt description.
Give every form field a label.
Use semantic elements to mark page sections.
Use headings in order so the outline makes sense.
Make sure links describe where they go, not just click here.

sections.text: Captions on videos were made for people who cannot hear, but most people use them in noisy places or with the sound off. Accessibility quietly helps everyone.

terms.definition: Making pages usable by everyone, including people with disabilities.
A tool that reads the page aloud.
A short text description of an image.
Text that names a form field.
An element whose name describes its part of the page.

options: Making pages usable by everyone, including people with disabilities
questions.explanation: Accessibility means everyone can use the page, including people with disabilities.

options: A tool that reads the page aloud
questions.explanation: A screen reader is a tool that reads the page aloud for people who cannot see it.
Clear alt text lets screen readers describe images, which improves accessibility.
